results.title: I saw someone post about how they hate debugging, and how it frustrates them and demotivates them. I feel the absolute opposite: I *love* debugging. I love taking a problem and digging into it… | Christopher Swenson | 12 comments
results.content:
🛠️ Debugging has taught me more patience than any textbook ever could. Ever spent hours battling a stubborn bug, gave up for the day… and then BOOM 💡 the solution appeared in your dream? Happens to me more often than I would like to admit. Sometimes I think my subconscious is a better debugger than I am 😂 Every developer knows the feeling: You spend hours chasing down a bug. Your code sits there, mocking you silently. Nothing works. 👉 The app crashes. 👉 The logic looks flawless. 👉 You have stared at the same 20 lines ten times... and still can't see it. Frustrating? Absolutely. But debugging is also one of our best teachers. It's taught me: 1. Patience → Rushing only drags out the process. 2. Attention to detail → A missing semicolon or wrong type can break everything. 3. Resilience → [...] How Debug Logs Supercharge Development Efficiency   elch78.github.io

  Debugging is emotional intelligence disguised as a technical skill. When I started coding, every bug felt personal. I’d rush into fixes, rewrite whole sections, and sometimes make things worse. Over time, I realized that debugging is mostly about how you think, not what you know. Calm developers see patterns. Anxious developers chase ghosts. The moment I stopped seeing bugs as “mistakes” and started seeing them as “messages,” my workflow transformed. Stay patient. Ask, “Why is the code doing exactly what I told it to?” — not “Why is this broken?” Debugging is communication with logic. Learn its language. [...] Chaitanya Rai

However, when you debug your code, you actually get a sense of accomplishment. You start to feel better and more confident about your code and the robustness of your program. Debugging, in some ways, can be a therapeutic process.

Bugs are chaotic, unexpected, unintentional, and often seem random. But there is a way to approach them that is intentional, methodical, and systematic!

In this video:
✅ 3 common bug categories
✅ Strategies on how to address them

When you've been coding for some time, you'll eventually run into problems in your code or bugs in your program. Even the most experienced programmers encounter bugs. Bugs are annoying because you don’t understand why they’re happening, which can be frustrating.

This is probably the most frustrating part of coding.

results.title: The Real Horror of Debugging a Code-based Application | Unqork Enterprise Application Development Platform
results.content:
Computer scientist Edsger Dijkstra once said, “If debugging is the process of removing software bugs, then programming must be the process of putting them in.” Ouch! Unfortunately, having to constantly find and fix programming errors is inevitable with traditional development methods. In fact, studies have shown that coders spend a staggering 75% of their development time just locating and squashing bugs.

Many of us have a vague idea of how time-consuming and frustrating debugging can be, but what do we really know? Let’s take a look at what debugging entails, and explore a better way to beat bugs using no-code.

## What is debugging, really?

> You could say the debugging process begins as soon as code is written and continues ad infinitum. [...] Debugging is the multi-step process of identifying a problem in your software, isolating it, and finding a way to either correct the issue or work around it. You could say the debugging process begins as soon as code is written and continues ad infinitum. Of course, you must fix bugs and remove buggy code whenever possible and as quickly as you can, but that’s far easier said than done. There’s no easy answer to how the debugging process works, nor is there a one-size-fits-all debugging solution. [...] Logic errors, on the other hand, are some of the hardest bugs to spot. This is because the program is technically correct and everything is working as expected, but you programmed the computer to do the wrong thing. Examples of logic errors include arithmetic errors (such as prompting an equation to divide by zero), misconfiguration of components like APIs, and more. If you’re lucky, the negative effect will be easy to reproduce so you can work backward and figure out what went wrong—and, oftentimes, it is not.

For example, the component renders twice. The API returns correct data. These are the things that you can prove about the code which is breaking. Right now this functions receives a string not a number. These are the facts that you need to note down. After knowing the fact about the code, then you go for the assumptions. Every bug is a broken assumption and most of your bugs live in this phase where you figure out what you assumed about this code. You assume something as a number. You assume that the effect runs once. You assume that the state updates immediately. These are your assumptions. The moment you get an assumption, your real debugging begins. Now you need something called a hypothesis to prove whether your assumption is right or your assumption is wrong. If I run the same code on production, I'll see just one call. So I solved the problem. Now one last time going back through this framework. This is nothing complex. I have broken down into multiple steps just to make you realize that debugging is never a guessing game. results.title: How to Troubleshoot Technical Issues (5 Steps) - Helply
results.content:
Nobody documented the fix after the first time. So the wheel gets reinvented, again and again. As one support lead put it in a SaaS operations thread: "Without a single source of truth, data gets lost between messages, agents, and teams, forcing the customer to explain their issue over and over." Another framed it even more bluntly: "If the same complaint pops up 5 times in a week, that's not a support problem. It's a documentation gap."

That documentation gap costs your team hours every month in duplicate work, slower resolution times, and frustrated customers who start wondering whether they picked the right product. [...] What was the expected outcome? Example: the customer clicks the "Export" button and a CSV file downloads.

What was the actual outcome? Example: the customer clicks "Export" and sees a 500 error, or nothing happens, or the page freezes.

This framing forces specificity. "It's broken" gives you nothing actionable. "I expected a CSV download and instead got an error" hands you the starting coordinates for diagnosis. [...] You have seen this ticket before. Not this exact one, but the pattern: "It's broken." No screenshot. No error message. No context. You ask clarifying questions. The customer replies six hours later: "Never mind, it's working now." Meanwhile, three other customers hit the exact same integration error this week, and each time a different agent handled it from scratch.
